statement: The three coalition parties' positions on the EU referendum as of March 2026: Samfylkingin (Social Democrats, 13 seats) is explicitly pro-EU membership and has been the primary driver of the referendum. Under PM Kristrún Frostadóttir, the party frames EU membership as essential for economic stability, security, and Iceland's place in Europe. Viðreisn (Reform Party, 11 seats) is the most consistently pro-EU party, founded in part on a pro-EU platform. Foreign Minister Þorgerður Katrín Gunnarsdóttir leads the party and the government's EU engagement. Flokkur fólksins (People's Party, 8 seats) is EU-sceptic but agreed to the referendum as part of the coalition agreement. The party is not campaigning for EU membership and may allow its members to vote freely. This internal tension — two pro-EU parties plus one sceptical partner — is the defining dynamic of the coalition's EU strategy.
source_name: Coalition agreement (stjórnarsáttmáli) 2024; party platforms; media reporting March 2026
caveats: Coalition dynamics could shift if the referendum result goes against the government's position. Flokkur fólksins' participation in a pro-EU coalition while opposing membership creates a credibility tension for both sides. The coalition's durability may depend on keeping the EU question compartmentalised from other policy areas.

related_entries.statement: The Althingi (Icelandic parliament) has 63 seats. As of session 157, 57 seats are held by sitting MPs. The party breakdown is: Sjálfstæðisflokkur (Independence Party) 13, Samfylkingin (Social Democrats) 13, Viðreisn (Reform Party) 11, Miðflokkurinn (Centre Party) 8, Flokkur fólksins (People's Party) 8, Framsóknarflokkur (Progressive Party) 4. The remaining 6 seats are vacant or held by alternates (varamenn).

The current Icelandic government is a coalition of Samfylkingin (Social Democrats, 13 seats), Viðreisn (Reform Party, 11 seats), and Flokkur fólksins (People's Party, 8 seats), holding 32 of 63 Althingi seats. The Prime Minister is Kristrún Frostadóttir (Samfylkingin). This is the first government coalition where both explicitly pro-EU parties (Samfylkingin and Viðreisn) serve together, which has been cited as a factor in advancing the EU referendum.

As of March 2026, the main opposition parties' positions on the EU referendum are: Sjálfstæðisflokkurinn (Independence Party, 13 seats) opposes EU membership but has not called for boycotting the referendum. The party's official position emphasises sovereignty, control of natural resources (especially fisheries), and the adequacy of the EEA arrangement. Sjálfstæðisflokkurinn has criticised the 29 August referendum date as unsuitable, arguing meaningful public debate cannot occur during summer holidays. The party's former leader submitted parliamentary resolutions six times to withdraw Iceland's EU application entirely. Miðflokkurinn (Centre Party, 8 seats) is firmly opposed to EU membership and has been the most vocally anti-EU party in Alþingi, consistently arguing against both membership and the referendum process. Framsóknarflokkurinn (Progressive Party, 4 seats) is internally divided but historically EU-sceptic; approximately half of its voters oppose continuing negotiations.

citations.report_title: Að kljúfa þjóðina með tilfinningaþrunginni spurningu

claims.claim_text: Kristrún Frostadóttir sagði að aðildarferli krefðist ofboðslega sterks umboðs og víðtækrar samstöðu í samfélaginu.
Þrjár stjórnarflokkar náðu samstöðu um að leita til þjóðarinnar í ESB-málinu.
